examples.explanation: Start with row=0: Try col=0: is_safe(0,0) true (no previous queens). Place queen at (0,0). Recurse to row=1: Try col=0: is_safe(1,0) false (same column as row0). Try col=1: is_safe(1,1) false (diagonal with row0: |0-1|==|0-1|). Try col=2: is_safe(1,2) true. Place queen at (1,2). Recurse to row=2: Try col=0: is_safe(2,0) false (diagonal with row1: |2-0|==|1-2|). Try col=1: is_safe(2,1) false (diagonal with row0: |0-1|==|0-2|). Try col=2: is_safe(2,2) false (same column as row1). Try col=3: is_safe(2,3) false (diagonal with row1: |2-3|==|1-2|). Backtrack to row=1, try col=3: is_safe(1,3) true. Place queen at (1,3). Recurse to row=2: Try col=0: is_safe(2,0) false (diagonal with row1: |3-0|==|1-2|). Try col=1: is_safe(2,1) true. Place queen at (2,1). Recurse to row=3: Try col=0: is_safe(3,0) false (diagonal with row2: |1-0|==|2-3|). Try col=1: is_safe(3,1) false (same column as row2). Try col=2: is_safe(3,2) false (diagonal with row1: |3-2|==|1-3|). Try col=3: is_safe(3,3) false (same column as row1). Backtrack to row=2, no more columns, backtrack to row=1, no more columns, backtrack to row=0. Try col=1: is_safe(0,1) true. Place queen at (0,1). Recurse to row=1: Try col=0: is_safe(1,0) false (diagonal with row0: |1-0|==|0-1|). Try col=1: is_safe(1,1) false (same column as row0). Try col=2: is_safe(1,2) false (diagonal with row0: |1-2|==|0-1|). Try col=3: is_safe(1,3) true. Place queen at (1,3). Recurse to row=2: Try col=0: is_safe(2,0) true. Place queen at (2,0). Recurse to row=3: Try col=0: is_safe(3,0) false (same column as row2). Try col=1: is_safe(3,1) false (diagonal with row2: |0-1|==|2-3|). Try col=2: is_safe(3,2) true. Place queen at (3,2). Row=4 reached, increment solutions to 1. Backtrack to row=3, try col=3: is_safe(3,3) false (diagonal with row1: |3-3|==|1-3|). Backtrack to row=2, try col=1: is_safe(2,1) false (diagonal with row1: |3-1|==|1-2|). Try col=2: is_safe(2,2) false (diagonal with row0: |1-2|==|0-2|). Try col=3: is_safe(2,3) false (same column as row1). Backtrack to row=1, no more columns, backtrack to row=0. Try col=2: is_safe(0,2) true. Place queen at (0,2). Recurse to row=1: Try col=0: is_safe(1,0) true. Place queen at (1,0). Recurse to row=2: Try col=0: is_safe(2,0) false (same column as row1). Try col=1: is_safe(2,1) false (diagonal with row1: |0-1|==|1-2|). Try col=2: is_safe(2,2) false (same column as row0). Try col=3: is_safe(2,3) true. Place queen at (2,3). Recurse to row=3: Try col=0: is_safe(3,0) false (diagonal with row2: |3-0|==|2-3|). Try col=1: is_safe(3,1) true. Place queen at (3,1). Row=4 reached, increment solutions to 2. Continue exploring other branches yields no more solutions. Total solutions: 2.
Start with row=0: Try col=0: is_safe(0,0) true (no previous queens). Place queen at (0,0). Recurse to row=1: row==N (1), increment solutions to 1. Backtrack, no more columns. Total solutions: 1.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Backtracking with O(N!) Time
approaches.summary: Place queens row by row, trying all columns and checking for conflicts with previous queens using row, column, and diagonal checks. Backtrack when a conflict is found to explore all possible board configurations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count N-Queens Solutions with Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to count all distinct solutions to the N-Queens problem using brute force backtracking.
ladders.explanation: The N-Queens problem requires placing N queens on an N×N chessboard such that no two queens threaten each other. Queens attack along rows, columns, and diagonals. The brute force backtracking approach systematically explores all possible placements row by row, eliminating invalid configurations early to avoid unnecessary exploration. Intuition: Since queens cannot share rows, we place exactly one queen per row. For each row, we try all columns, checking if placing a queen there conflicts with previously placed queens. If valid, we recurse to the next row; if invalid, we backtrack. This reduces the search space from N^(N^2) to roughly N! by pruning invalid branches. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize a board representation (e.g., array of column positions per row). 2. Define a function is_safe(row, col) that checks conflicts with previous queens using column and diagonal checks (|col1 - col2| != |row1 - row2| for diagonals). 3. Recursive backtrack function: if row == N, increment solution count; else for each column c, if is_safe(row, c), place queen and recurse to row+1. Time: O(N!) in worst case as we explore permutations of column placements. Space: O(N) for recursion stack and board storage. Connection to L1: This integrates the is_safe check (L1) into the full backtracking framework. Common mistakes: Not resetting board state after recursion (use backtracking properly), incorrect diagonal check (use absolute difference), or missing base case. Real code pattern: def solve_n_queens(N): solutions = 0; board = [-1]*N; def is_safe(row, col): for r in range(row): if board[r] == col or abs(board[r] - col) == abs(r - row): return False; return True; def backtrack(row): nonlocal solutions; if row == N: solutions += 1; return; for c in range(N): if is_safe(row, c): board[row] = c; backtrack(row+1); board[row] = -1; backtrack(0); return solutions.

examples.trace: Start with row=0: Try col=0: is_safe(0,0) true (no previous queens). Place queen at (0,0). Recurse to row=1: Try col=0: is_safe(1,0) false (same column as row0). Try col=1: is_safe(1,1) false (diagonal with row0: |0-1|==|0-1|). Try col=2: is_safe(1,2) true. Place queen at (1,2). Recurse to row=2: Try col=0: is_safe(2,0) false (diagonal with row1: |2-0|==|1-2|). Try col=1: is_safe(2,1) false (diagonal with row0: |0-1|==|0-2|). Try col=2: is_safe(2,2) false (same column as row1). Try col=3: is_safe(2,3) false (diagonal with row1: |2-3|==|1-2|). Backtrack to row=1, try col=3: is_safe(1,3) true. Place queen at (1,3). Recurse to row=2: Try col=0: is_safe(2,0) false (diagonal with row1: |3-0|==|1-2|). Try col=1: is_safe(2,1) true. Place queen at (2,1). Recurse to row=3: Try col=0: is_safe(3,0) false (diagonal with row2: |1-0|==|2-3|). Try col=1: is_safe(3,1) false (same column as row2). Try col=2: is_safe(3,2) false (diagonal with row1: |3-2|==|1-3|). Try col=3: is_safe(3,3) false (same column as row1). Backtrack to row=2, no more columns, backtrack to row=1, no more columns, backtrack to row=0. Try col=1: is_safe(0,1) true. Place queen at (0,1). Recurse to row=1: Try col=0: is_safe(1,0) false (diagonal with row0: |1-0|==|0-1|). Try col=1: is_safe(1,1) false (same column as row0). Try col=2: is_safe(1,2) false (diagonal with row0: |1-2|==|0-1|). Try col=3: is_safe(1,3) true. Place queen at (1,3). Recurse to row=2: Try col=0: is_safe(2,0) true. Place queen at (2,0). Recurse to row=3: Try col=0: is_safe(3,0) false (same column as row2). Try col=1: is_safe(3,1) false (diagonal with row2: |0-1|==|2-3|). Try col=2: is_safe(3,2) true. Place queen at (3,2). Row=4 reached, increment solutions to 1. Backtrack to row=3, try col=3: is_safe(3,3) false (diagonal with row1: |3-3|==|1-3|). Backtrack to row=2, try col=1: is_safe(2,1) false (diagonal with row1: |3-1|==|1-2|). Try col=2: is_safe(2,2) false (diagonal with row0: |1-2|==|0-2|). Try col=3: is_safe(2,3) false (same column as row1). Backtrack to row=1, no more columns, backtrack to row=0. Try col=2: is_safe(0,2) true. Place queen at (0,2). Recurse to row=1: Try col=0: is_safe(1,0) true. Place queen at (1,0). Recurse to row=2: Try col=0: is_safe(2,0) false (same column as row1). Try col=1: is_safe(2,1) false (diagonal with row1: |0-1|==|1-2|). Try col=2: is_safe(2,2) false (same column as row0). Try col=3: is_safe(2,3) true. Place queen at (2,3). Recurse to row=3: Try col=0: is_safe(3,0) false (diagonal with row2: |3-0|==|2-3|). Try col=1: is_safe(3,1) true. Place queen at (3,1). Row=4 reached, increment solutions to 2. Continue exploring other branches yields no more solutions. Total solutions: 2.
Start with row=0: Try col=0: is_safe(0,0) true (no previous queens). Place queen at (0,0). Recurse to row=1: row==N (1), increment solutions to 1. Backtrack, no more columns. Total solutions: 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Safe Placement Check for Queens
ladders.desc: Implement a function to check if placing a queen at a given position is safe relative to previously placed queens.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is crucial for pruning invalid configurations in backtracking. Intuition: A new queen at (row, col) must not share a column or diagonal with any previously placed queen. Since we place queens row by row, we only need to check queens in rows 0 to row-1. The check ensures: 1. No two queens in same column: column positions must differ. 2. No two queens on same diagonal: The absolute difference in columns equals the absolute difference in rows for queens on the same diagonal (slope ±1). Step-by-step: Given board array where board[i] stores column of queen in row i (or -1 if no queen), for a new queen at (row, col), iterate i from 0 to row-1: if board[i] == col (same column) or abs(board[i] - col) == abs(i - row) (same diagonal), return false; else return true after loop. Time: O(N) per check as we check up to N previous queens. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called for each placement attempt in the backtracking algorithm. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check all previous rows, incorrect diagonal condition (must use absolute values), or off-by-one errors in loop bounds. Real code pattern: def is_safe(board, row, col): for i in range(row): if board[i] == col or abs(board[i] - col) == abs(i - row): return False; return True.

examples.trace: Board has queens at row0 col0 and row1 col2. Check row=2, col=1: Iterate i=0: board[0]=0, check column: 0==1? false; diagonal: |0-1|==|0-2|? 1==2? false. i=1: board[1]=2, check column: 2==1? false; diagonal: |2-1|==|1-2|? 1==1? true → return false. Queen at (2,1) is on same diagonal as queen at (1,2).

Board has queens at row0 col1, row1 col3, row2 col0. Check row=3, col=2: Iterate i=0: board[0]=1, column: 1==2? false; diagonal: |1-2|==|0-3|? 1==3? false. i=1: board[1]=3, column: 3==2? false; diagonal: |3-2|==|1-3|? 1==2? false. i=2: board[2]=0, column: 0==2? false; diagonal: |0-2|==|2-3|? 2==1? false. All checks passed, return true. Queen at (3,2) is safe.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking Search for Permutations
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive backtracking algorithm that explores all valid permutations of queen placements row by row.
ladders.explanation: This is the engine of the solution: systematically trying all possible column placements per row and backtracking when conflicts arise. Intuition: Since each row must have exactly one queen, we can think of placing queens as choosing a permutation of column indices for rows 0 to N-1, subject to safety constraints. Backtracking explores this permutation space efficiently by pruning invalid partial placements early. Step-by-step: 1. Define a recursive function backtrack(row) that attempts to place a queen in row. 2. Base case: if row == N, a valid placement is complete (increment solution count). 3. Recursive case: for each column c from 0 to N-1, if placing a queen at (row, c) is safe (using is_safe), place it (update board), recurse to backtrack(row+1), then remove it (backtrack). This explores all possibilities depth-first. Time: O(N!) in worst case as we generate permutations of N columns with pruning. Space: O(N) for recursion depth and board storage. Connection to L1: Uses is_safe to validate each placement attempt. Common mistakes: Not unplacing queen after recursion (leading to state corruption), missing base case, or incorrect loop range. Real code pattern: def backtrack(board, row, N): if row == N: increment count; return; for col in range(N): if is_safe(board, row, col): board[row] = col; backtrack(board, row+1, N); board[row] = -1.

examples.trace: Initialize board=[-1,-1,-1]. backtrack(0): row=0 not N, try col=0: is_safe true, board[0]=0, call backtrack(1). backtrack(1): try col=0: is_safe false (same column as row0). col=1: is_safe false (diagonal with row0: |0-1|==|0-1|). col=2: is_safe true, board[1]=2, call backtrack(2). backtrack(2): try col=0: is_safe false (diagonal with row1: |2-0|==|1-2|). col=1: is_safe false (diagonal with row0: |0-1|==|0-2|). col=2: is_safe false (same column as row1). No valid col, return to backtrack(1). backtrack(1): no more cols, return to backtrack(0). backtrack(0): try col=1: is_safe true, board[0]=1, call backtrack(1). backtrack(1): try col=0: is_safe false (diagonal with row0: |1-0|==|0-1|). col=1: is_safe false (same column as row0). col=2: is_safe false (diagonal with row0: |1-2|==|0-1|). No valid col, return to backtrack(0). backtrack(0): try col=2: is_safe true, board[0]=2, call backtrack(1). backtrack(1): try col=0: is_safe true, board[1]=0, call backtrack(2). backtrack(2): try col=0: is_safe false (same column as row1). col=1: is_safe false (diagonal with row1: |0-1|==|1-2|). col=2: is_safe false (same column as row0). No valid col, return to backtrack(1). backtrack(1): try col=1: is_safe false (diagonal with row0: |2-1|==|0-1|). col=2: is_safe false (same column as row0). No valid col, return to backtrack(0). backtrack(0): no more cols, end. No solutions found.

Initialize board=[-1,-1]. backtrack(0): row=0 not N, try col=0: is_safe true, board[0]=0, call backtrack(1). backtrack(1): try col=0: is_safe false (same column as row0). col=1: is_safe false (diagonal with row0: |0-1|==|0-1|). No valid col, return to backtrack(0). backtrack(0): try col=1: is_safe true, board[0]=1, call backtrack(1). backtrack(1): try col=0: is_safe false (diagonal with row0: |1-0|==|0-1|). col=1: is_safe false (same column as row0). No valid col, return to backtrack(0). backtrack(0): no more cols, end. No solutions found.

testCases.expected: One path: place at col0, count=1
Continue search from row2, may find solutions
Continue search from row3, may find solutions

ladders.title: Building Block — Representing and Updating Queen Placements
ladders.desc: Create a data structure to track queen positions and update it during backtracking.
ladders.explanation: An efficient representation of the chessboard state is essential for backtracking. Intuition: We don't need a full N×N matrix; since we place one queen per row, we can store only the column position for each row. This reduces space and simplifies checks. Use an array board of size N, where board[row] = column of queen in that row, or a sentinel value (e.g., -1) if no queen placed yet. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize board with -1 for all rows. 2. When placing a queen at (row, col), set board[row] = col. 3. When removing a queen (backtracking), reset board[row] to -1. This allows is_safe to check only relevant rows. Time: O(1) for placement and removal. Space: O(N) for the array. Connection to L2: This board is passed to the backtracking function and updated recursively. Common mistakes: Using a 2D array unnecessarily (wastes space), not resetting to -1 on backtrack (causes state errors), or incorrect indexing. Real code pattern: board = [-1] * N; def place_queen(row, col): board[row] = col; def remove_queen(row): board[row] = -1.

examples.input: N=4, place queens at (0,1), (1,3), then remove (1,3)
examples.trace: Start: board = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Place queen at (0,1): board[0] = 1 → board = [1,-1,-1,-1]. Place queen at (1,3): board[1] = 3 → board = [1,3,-1,-1]. Remove queen at (1,3): board[1] = -1 → board = [1,-1,-1,-1].

examples.input: N=3, place queens at (0,0), (1,2), then remove (0,0)
examples.trace: Start: board = [-1,-1,-1]. Place queen at (0,0): board[0] = 0 → board = [0,-1,-1]. Place queen at (1,2): board[1] = 2 → board = [0,2,-1]. Remove queen at (0,0): board[0] = -1 → board = [-1,2,-1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking Diagonal Conflicts
ladders.desc: Determine if two queens are on the same diagonal using their row and column coordinates.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks whether two queens threaten each other diagonally. Intuition: Two points (r1,c1) and (r2,c2) are on the same diagonal if the slope between them is ±1, i.e., |c1 - c2| == |r1 - r2|. This arises because diagonals on a chessboard have a constant difference or sum of coordinates, but for the main check, absolute differences suffice. Step-by-step: Given two queens at positions (r1,c1) and (r2,c2), compute diff_col = |c1 - c2| and diff_row = |r1 - r2|. If diff_col == diff_row, they are on the same diagonal (either main or anti-diagonal). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This check is used within is_safe when comparing a new queen with previous ones. Common mistakes: Using sum instead of absolute difference, or forgetting to handle both positive and negative slopes. Real code pattern: def on_same_diagonal(r1, c1, r2, c2): return abs(c1 - c2) == abs(r1 - r2).

examples.trace: Compute diff_col = |0-1| = 1, diff_row = |0-1| = 1. Since 1 == 1, they are on the same diagonal (main diagonal).
Compute diff_col = |3-1| = 2, diff_row = |2-4| = 2. Since 2 == 2, they are on the same diagonal (anti-diagonal).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Chessboard Coordinates and Queen Movement
ladders.desc: Learn how rows, columns, and diagonals work on a chessboard and how queens attack.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept is essential for grasping the N-Queens problem. Intuition: A chessboard is an N×N grid with rows (horizontal lines) and columns (vertical lines). Rows are typically numbered 0 to N-1 from top to bottom, columns 0 to N-1 from left to right. A queen attacks any piece in the same row, same column, or same diagonal (both main and anti-diagonals). Step-by-step: 1. Same row: Two pieces have the same row index. 2. Same column: Two pieces have the same column index. 3. Same diagonal: The absolute difference in row indices equals the absolute difference in column indices. Visualize: Main diagonals run top-left to bottom-right (row increase = column increase), anti-diagonals run top-right to bottom-left (row increase = column decrease). Time: O(1) to understand. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This explains why the diagonal check works. Common mistakes: Confusing rows and columns, or misunderstanding diagonal slopes. Real code pattern: Conceptual understanding only; no code needed at this level.

examples.input: On a 4x4 board, identify if queen at (1,2) attacks (3,0)
examples.trace: Positions: (1,2) and (3,0). Check same row: 1 != 3, no. Same column: 2 != 0, no. Diagonal: |1-3| = 2, |2-0| = 2, equal, so on same diagonal (anti-diagonal). Thus, queen attacks.

examples.input: On a 4x4 board, identify if queen at (0,0) attacks (2,1)
examples.trace: Positions: (0,0) and (2,1). Check same row: 0 != 2, no. Same column: 0 != 1, no. Diagonal: |0-2| = 2, |0-1| = 1, not equal, so not on same diagonal. Thus, queen does not attack.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Optimized Backtracking with Bitmasking O(N!)
approaches.summary: Use bitmasking to track occupied columns and diagonals efficiently, allowing constant-time conflict checks. This reduces the overhead of checking each queen's position individually.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count N-Queens Solutions with Bitmasking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation counting all valid N-Queens placements using bitmasking for efficient conflict checking.
ladders.explanation: This solution counts all distinct placements of N queens on an N×N chessboard where no two queens threaten each other, using optimized backtracking with bitmasking. The intuition is to place queens row by row while tracking occupied columns and diagonals using bitmasks, allowing O(1) conflict checks. We maintain three bitmasks: columns (bits represent occupied columns), left diagonals (bits represent occupied left diagonals), and right diagonals (bits represent occupied right diagonals). For each row, we iterate through available columns where placing a queen doesn't conflict with existing queens. When placing a queen at (row, col), we update the bitmasks: columns sets bit col, left diagonals sets bit (row - col + N - 1) to handle negative indices, and right diagonals sets bit (row + col). We then recursively process the next row. When we reach row N, we've placed all queens successfully and increment the count. The time complexity is O(N!) because each row reduces available positions, though bitmasking reduces constant factors significantly. Space complexity is O(N) for recursion depth and bitmask storage. Connection to L1: This integrates the backtracking function with bitmask updates to count all solutions. Common mistakes include incorrect diagonal bitmask calculations (off-by-one errors with negative indices) and forgetting to unset bits during backtracking. Real code pattern: def totalNQueens(n): def backtrack(row, cols, diag1, diag2): if row == n: return 1 count = 0 available = ~(cols | diag1 | diag2) & ((1 << n) - 1) while available: col_bit = available & -available col = (col_bit.bit_length() - 1) count += backtrack(row+1, cols|col_bit, (diag1|col_bit) << 1, (diag2|col_bit) >> 1) available &= available - 1 return count return backtrack(0, 0, 0, 0)

examples.trace:
Initial: row=0, cols=0b0, diag1=0b0, diag2=0b0
Row 0: available=~0 & 0b1=0b1
  Try col 0: col_bit=0b1, new cols=0b1, new diag1=(0|1)<<1=0b10 (2-bit), new diag2=(0|1)>>1=0b0
  Recursive call row=1
    Row 1 reached → count=1
Total count=1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking Function with Bitmask Updates
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive backtracking function that places queens row by row using bitmask operations.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that explores all valid queen placements. The intuition is to treat each row as a decision point where we choose a column for the queen, ensuring no conflicts with previously placed queens using bitmask checks. We maintain three integer bitmasks: cols (occupied columns), diag1 (occupied left diagonals), and diag2 (occupied right diagonals). For the current row, we compute available positions by taking the complement of the union of all occupied bits, then mask with (1<<n)-1 to keep only n bits. We iterate through each available column using low-bit extraction: while available: col_bit = available & -available (extracts lowest set bit), col = bit_position(col_bit), then recursively call for next row with updated bitmasks. When updating: new_cols = cols | col_bit, new_diag1 = (diag1 | col_bit) << 1 (left diagonals shift down), new_diag2 = (diag2 | col_bit) >> 1 (right diagonals shift up). The shift operations account for diagonal movement to the next row. When row == n, we return 1 (successful placement). Time complexity per call is O(n) for bit operations, but overall O(N!) due to recursive calls. Space is O(n) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called by the main solution to count all placements. Common mistakes: incorrect bitmask sizes (not masking with n bits), forgetting to shift diagonals, or incorrect base case. Real code pattern: def backtrack(row, cols, diag1, diag2, n): if row == n: return 1 count = 0 available = ~(cols | diag1 | diag2) & ((1 << n) - 1) while available: col_bit = available & -available col = (col_bit.bit_length() - 1) count += backtrack(row+1, cols|col_bit, (diag1|col_bit)<<1, (diag2|col_bit)>>1, n) available &= available - 1 return count

examples.trace:
Row 0: available=~0 & 0b111=0b111
  Try col 0: col_bit=0b001, recursive call row=1 with cols=0b001, diag1=(0|1)<<1=0b010, diag2=(0|1)>>1=0b000
    Row 1: available=~(0b001|0b010|0b000)&0b111=~0b011&0b111=0b100
    Try col 2: col_bit=0b100, recursive call row=2 with cols=0b101, diag1=(0b010|0b100)<<1=0b110<<1=0b100, diag2=(0b000|0b100)>>1=0b100>>1=0b010
      Row 2: available=~(0b101|0b100|0b010)&0b111=~0b111&0b111=0b000 → return 0
    Return 0
  Try col 1: col_bit=0b010, recursive call row=1 with cols=0b010, diag1=(0|2)<<1=0b100, diag2=(0|2)>>1=0b001
    Row 1: available=~(0b010|0b100|0b001)&0b111=~0b111&0b111=0b000 → return 0
  Try col 2: col_bit=0b100, recursive call row=1 with cols=0b100, diag1=(0|4)<<1=0b000 (5-bit truncated), diag2=(0|4)>>1=0b010
    Row 1: available=~(0b100|0b000|0b010)&0b111=~0b110&0b111=0b001
    Try col 0: col_bit=0b001, recursive call row=2 with cols=0b101, diag1=(0b000|1)<<1=0b001<<1=0b010, diag2=(0b010|1)>>1=0b011>>1=0b001
      Row 2: available=~(0b101|0b010|0b001)&0b111=~0b111&0b111=0b000 → return 0
    Return 0
  Return 0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask Conflict Checking for Queens
ladders.desc: Use bitmask operations to efficiently check if a queen placement conflicts with existing queens.
ladders.explanation: This technique uses bitwise operations to track occupied columns and diagonals, enabling O(1) conflict checks. The intuition is to represent the chessboard state as three integer bitmasks where each bit corresponds to a column or diagonal line that is threatened by already placed queens. For an N×N board, we use N-bit integers. The columns mask has bit i set if column i is occupied. The left diagonals mask (diagonal from top-left to bottom-right) uses the property that cells on the same left diagonal have constant (row - col). We map this to bit index (row - col + N - 1) to avoid negative indices. The right diagonals mask (diagonal from top-right to bottom-left) uses constant (row + col), mapped to bit index (row + col). When placing a queen at (row, col), we check if bit col in columns, bit (row-col+N-1) in left diagonals, or bit (row+col) in right diagonals is set—if any is set, there's a conflict. To check availability: available = ~(cols | diag1 | diag2) & ((1 << n) - 1). The bitwise OR combines all threats, NOT inverts to get safe positions, and masking keeps only n bits. Time complexity is O(1) per check. Space is O(1) for integers. Connection to L1: This logic is used in the backtracking function to compute available positions. Common mistakes: incorrect diagonal indexing, not masking bits, or using wrong bit operations. Real code pattern: def is_safe(row, col, cols, diag1, diag2, n): if cols & (1 << col): return False if diag1 & (1 << (row - col + n - 1)): return False if diag2 & (1 << (row + col)): return False return True

examples.input: row=1, col=2, cols=0b0010, diag1=0b0100, diag2=0b0001, n=4
examples.trace:
Check column: cols & (1<<2) = 0b0010 & 0b0100 = 0b0000 → safe
Check left diagonal: index = 1-2+4-1=2, diag1 & (1<<2) = 0b0100 & 0b0100 = 0b0100 → conflict! Actually wait, let's recompute: row=1, col=2, n=4. left diagonal index = row-col+n-1 = 1-2+3=2. diag1=0b0100 has bit 2 set? 0b0100 is binary for 4, which is bit 2 set (bits numbered 0 from right). So conflict exists. But output should be False. Let's correct: The example claims output True, but with diag1=0b0100 and index 2, there is a conflict. Let's use a correct example: row=1, col=3, cols=0b0010, diag1=0b0100, diag2=0b0001, n=4. Check column: cols & (1<<3)=0b0010&0b1000=0 → safe. Left diagonal: index=1-3+3=1, diag1 & (1<<1)=0b0100&0b0010=0 → safe. Right diagonal: index=1+3=4, but n=4 so max index 6? Actually right diagonal uses row+col, which can go up to 2n-2=6 for n=4. diag2=0b0001 has bit 0 set, not bit 4 → safe. So output True.

examples.input: row=0, col=0, cols=0b0000, diag1=0b0000, diag2=0b0000, n=4
examples.trace:
Check column: cols & (1<<0)=0b0000&0b0001=0 → safe
Check left diagonal: index=0-0+3=3, diag1 & (1<<3)=0b0000&0b1000=0 → safe
Check right diagonal: index=0+0=0, diag2 & (1<<0)=0b0000&0b0001=0 → safe
All checks pass → True

testCases.input: row=0, col=0, cols=0, diag1=0, diag2=0, n=4
row=0, col=0, cols=1, diag1=0, diag2=0, n=4
row=1, col=1, cols=0, diag1=2, diag2=0, n=4
row=1, col=1, cols=0, diag1=0, diag2=2, n=4
row=2, col=1, cols=0, diag1=0, diag2=0, n=4
row=2, col=1, cols=2, diag1=0, diag2=0, n=4
row=3, col=3, cols=0, diag1=0, diag2=0, n=4
row=3, col=3, cols=8, diag1=0, diag2=0, n=4
row=1, col=2, cols=0, diag1=0, diag2=0, n=4
row=1, col=2, cols=0, diag1=4, diag2=0, n=4

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitmask Representation of Diagonals
ladders.desc: Map chessboard diagonals to integer bitmasks for efficient tracking.
ladders.explanation: This concept involves representing the two diagonal directions on a chessboard as bitmasks to track which diagonals are occupied by queens. The intuition is that each diagonal can be uniquely identified by an index, and we can use a bit in an integer to indicate if that diagonal is threatened. For left diagonals (top-left to bottom-right), all cells satisfy row - col = constant. This constant ranges from -(N-1) to (N-1). To map to non-negative bit indices, we use index = row - col + N - 1, which ranges from 0 to 2N-2. For right diagonals (top-right to bottom-left), all cells satisfy row + col = constant, ranging from 0 to 2N-2, so index = row + col. We maintain two integers, diag1 and diag2, each with at least 2N-1 bits. When a queen is placed at (row, col), we set bit index in diag1 and diag2. For example, on a 4×4 board, left diagonal indices: cell (0,0) gives 0-0+3=3, cell (1,2) gives 1-2+3=2. Right diagonal indices: cell (0,0) gives 0, cell (1,2) gives 3. Time complexity for updating is O(1) bit operations. Space is O(1) for integers. Connection to L2: These masks are used in conflict checking. Common mistakes: incorrect offset calculation for left diagonals, or not accounting for bit integer size. Real code pattern: left_diag_index = row - col + n - 1; right_diag_index = row + col; diag1_mask = 1 << left_diag_index; diag2_mask = 1 << right_diag_index.

examples.trace:
For left diagonal: row - col + n - 1 = 1 - 2 + 4 - 1 = 2. So bit index 2 in diag1 mask.
For right diagonal: row + col = 1 + 2 = 3. So bit index 3 in diag2 mask.
Thus, diag1 gets bit 2 set (value 4), diag2 gets bit 3 set (value 8).

Left diagonal: 0 - 3 + 3 = 0. Bit index 0.
Right diagonal: 0 + 3 = 3. Bit index 3.
diag1: bit 0 (value 1), diag2: bit 3 (value 8).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitwise Manipulation for Set Operations
ladders.desc: Perform bitwise operations to manipulate sets of columns/diagonals.
ladders.explanation: This involves using bitwise operators to efficiently handle sets of indices. The intuition is to treat an integer as a bitset where each bit represents an element (e.g., a column or diagonal), with 1 meaning occupied and 0 meaning free. Key operations: OR (|) to combine sets (union), AND (&) to intersect sets, NOT (~) to complement, XOR (^) for symmetric difference, and shifts (<<, >>) to move bits. For example, to mark column i as occupied: mask = mask | (1 << i). To check if column i is occupied: (mask & (1 << i)) != 0. To get the lowest set bit: low_bit = mask & -mask (using two's complement). To iterate through set bits: while mask: bit = mask & -mask; mask &= mask - 1. Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space is O(1) for integers. Connection to L3: These operations are used to update and query diagonal bitmasks. Common mistakes: forgetting that integers have unlimited bits in Python but need masking for fixed size, or incorrect bit indexing. Real code pattern: cols = 0; cols |= (1 << 2)  # set bit 2; if cols & (1 << 2): print('occupied'); low_bit = cols & -cols; cols &= cols - 1  # clear lowest set bit.

examples.input: mask=0b1010, set bit 1, clear bit 3, get lowest set bit
examples.trace:
Initial mask: binary 1010 (bits 3 and 1 set).
Set bit 1: 1<<1 = 0010, mask | 0010 = 1010 | 0010 = 1010 (bit 1 already set).
Clear bit 3: ~(1<<3) = ~1000 = ...11110111, mask & ~1000 = 1010 & 0111 = 0010.
Lowest set bit: mask & -mask: mask=0010, -mask in two's complement is 1110 (for 4-bit), 0010 & 1110 = 0010.

mask=0110 (binary).
First iteration: low_bit = 0110 & -0110. -0110 in two's complement: 0110 is 6, -6 is ...11111010, so 0110 & 1010 = 0010 (bit 1, value 2). mask &= mask-1: 0110 & 0101 = 0100.
Second iteration: low_bit = 0100 & -0100. -0100 is ...11111100, 0100 & 1100 = 0100 (bit 2, value 4). mask &= mask-1: 0100 & 0011 = 0000.
Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Representation and Bitwise Operators
ladders.desc: Understand how integers are represented in binary and basic bitwise operations.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers binary number representation and bitwise operators. Integers in computers are stored in binary (base-2), where each bit is 0 or 1. For example, decimal 5 is binary 101 (1*4 + 0*2 + 1*1). Bitwise operators work on these bits: AND (&) returns 1 only if both bits are 1, OR (|) returns 1 if at least one bit is 1, XOR (^) returns 1 if bits differ, NOT (~) flips all bits, left shift (<<) shifts bits left adding 0s, right shift (>>) shifts bits right dropping bits. For example, 5 & 3 = 101 & 011 = 001 = 1. Shifting: 5 << 1 = 1010 = 10. These operations are fast and used to manipulate sets of flags or indices. Time complexity is O(1). Space is O(1). Connection to L4: This knowledge is prerequisite for bitmask manipulations. Common mistakes: confusing bitwise AND with logical AND, or not understanding two's complement for negative numbers. Real code pattern: a = 5  # binary 101; b = 3  # 011; c = a & b  # 001 = 1; d = a << 2  # 10100 = 20.

examples.trace:
5 in binary: 101.
Left shift by 1: 101 << 1 = 1010 = decimal 10.
Right shift by 1: 101 >> 1 = 010 = decimal 2.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Iterative Depth-First Search with Bitmask O(N!)
approaches.summary: Implement backtracking iteratively using a stack to simulate recursion, combined with bitmasking for efficient conflict checks. This avoids recursion overhead and can be more memory-efficient in some environments.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count N-Queens Solutions with Iterative DFS and Bitmask
ladders.desc: Complete implementation counting all valid N-Queens placements using iterative DFS with bitmask optimization.
ladders.explanation: This solution counts all distinct placements of N queens on an N×N chessboard where no two queens threaten each other. We use an iterative depth-first search (DFS) with a stack to simulate recursion, avoiding recursion overhead. Bitmasking optimizes conflict checks: we maintain three bitmasks for columns, left diagonals, and right diagonals. Each bit represents whether that column/diagonal is occupied. The stack stores states (row, col_mask, ld_mask, rd_mask, count). We iterate through rows from 0 to N-1. For each row, we try all columns where no conflicts exist (using bitwise operations). When placing a queen, we update masks and push the next state. When we reach row N, we increment the solution count. The intuition is that iterative DFS with explicit stack control gives us fine-grained memory management while bitmasking provides O(1) conflict checks. Time complexity is O(N!) in worst case as we explore all permutations, but pruning reduces it. Space complexity is O(N) for the stack depth. Connection to L1: This integrates the bitmask conflict checking subroutine into the complete iterative DFS framework. Common mistakes include incorrect bit shifting for diagonals or forgetting to backtrack masks properly in iterative approach. Real code pattern: Initialize stack with (0, 0, 0, 0, 0). While stack not empty: pop state. If row == N: increment count. Else: generate safe positions via bitwise ops. For each safe col: push (row+1, updated_masks, count).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize stack with [(0,0,0,0,0)], solutions=0. Step 2: Pop (0,0,0,0,0). Row=0<N=1. Safe cols = ~(0|0|0) & ((1<<1)-1=1) = 1 (col=0). Try col=0: col_mask=1, ld_mask=1<<(0-0+0)=1, rd_mask=1<<(0+0)=1, push (1,1,1,1,0). Step 3: Pop (1,1,1,1,0). Row=1==N, increment solutions to 1. Stack empty. Return solutions=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bitmask Conflict Checking for Queen Placement
ladders.desc: Determine safe columns for queen placement using bitmask operations.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine checks which columns in a given row are safe to place a queen, considering conflicts from previously placed queens. We use three bitmasks: col_mask (occupied columns), ld_mask (occupied left diagonals), rd_mask (occupied right diagonals). Each mask is an N-bit integer where bit i=1 means column/diagonal i is threatened. For a candidate column c in current row r: column conflict if col_mask has bit c set; left diagonal conflict if ld_mask has bit (c - r + N - 1) set (since left diagonals have constant c-r); right diagonal conflict if rd_mask has bit (c + r) set (since right diagonals have constant c+r). The safe positions are computed as: safe = ~(col_mask | ld_mask | rd_mask) & ((1 << N) - 1). The NOT flips bits to mark safe positions as 1, and the AND with (1<<N)-1 keeps only N bits. We then iterate through set bits in safe. Intuition: Bitmasking compresses N boolean checks into O(1) bitwise operations, dramatically speeding up conflict detection. Time: O(N) to extract bits, but often optimized with while safe > 0. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This is the core efficiency mechanism in the full solution. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in diagonal indexing or incorrect bit masking. Real code pattern: def get_safe_cols(row, col_mask, ld_mask, rd_mask, N): occupied = col_mask | ld_mask | rd_mask; safe = ~occupied & ((1 << N) - 1); return safe.

examples.trace: Step 1: Convert masks: col_mask=00101 (cols 0,2 occupied), ld_mask=01010 (left diags 1,3 threatened), rd_mask=00110 (right diags 1,2 threatened). Step 2: Compute occupied = 00101 | 01010 | 00110 = 01111 (bits 0,1,2,3 set). Step 3: safe = ~01111 & ((1<<5)-1=11111) = 10000 & 11111 = 10000. Step 4: safe=10000 has bit 4 set, so only column 4 is safe. Verification: For col=4: col conflict? col_mask bit4=0 ✓. Left diagonal index = 4-2+4=6, but N=5 so use modulo? Actually we use N-1 offset: 4-2+4=6, bit6 in ld_mask? ld_mask is 5-bit so bit6=0 ✓. Right diagonal index = 4+2=6, bit6 in rd_mask=0 ✓. So col4 safe.
Step 1: All masks zero. Step 2: occupied = 0 | 0 | 0 = 0. Step 3: safe = ~0 & (111) = 111. Step 4: safe=111 has bits 0,1,2 set, so all columns 0,1,2 are safe in first row.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative DFS with Stack for Backtracking
ladders.desc: Simulate depth-first search backtracking using an explicit stack instead of recursion.
ladders.explanation: This technique replaces recursive function calls with an explicit stack data structure to traverse the solution space. We push states onto the stack representing partial solutions, then pop and process them. For N-Queens, each state includes: current row, column mask, left diagonal mask, right diagonal mask, and possibly a count. The algorithm: initialize stack with starting state (row=0, masks=0). While stack not empty: pop state. If row == N: increment solution count (base case). Else: compute safe columns using bitmask subroutine. For each safe column: create new state with row+1 and updated masks, push onto stack. Intuition: Iterative DFS gives us explicit control over the stack, avoiding recursion overhead and potential stack overflow for deep recursion. It also allows for easier debugging and memory optimization. Time: O(N!) as we explore all placements. Space: O(N) for stack depth. Connection to L1: This framework uses the conflict checking subroutine at each step. Common mistakes: Forgetting to push states in correct order (LIFO) or incorrectly managing state variables. Real code pattern: stack = [(0,0,0,0)]; while stack: row, cm, lm, rm = stack.pop(); if row==N: count+=1; else: safe = get_safe_cols(...); while safe: col = get_lsb(safe); push (row+1, updated_masks); safe &= safe-1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize stack = [(0,0,0,0)], count=0. Step 2: Pop (0,0,0,0). Row=0<2. Safe cols = ~0 & 11 = 11 (cols 0,1). Try col=1 first (since stack LIFO, order may vary): push (1, 10, 01, 10) [masks in binary]. Try col=0: push (1, 01, 10, 01). Step 3: Pop (1,01,10,01). Row=1<2. Safe cols = ~(01|10|01) & 11 = ~11 & 11 = 00. No safe cols, backtrack. Step 4: Pop (1,10,01,10). Safe cols = ~(10|01|10) & 11 = ~11 & 11 = 00. No safe cols. Stack empty, count=0.

Step 1: stack = [(0,0,0,0)], count=0. Step 2: Pop (0,0,0,0). Row=0<1. Safe cols = ~0 & 1 = 1. Try col=0: push (1,1,1,1). Step 3: Pop (1,1,1,1). Row=1==1, increment count to 1. Stack empty. Return count=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitmask Representation of Board States
ladders.desc: Use integer bitmasks to compactly represent which columns and diagonals are occupied.
ladders.explanation: Bitmasking is a technique where we use the bits of an integer to represent boolean states. For N-Queens, we need to track occupied columns, left diagonals, and right diagonals. Each is represented as an N-bit integer for columns, and (2N-1)-bit for diagonals, but we often use N bits with indexing tricks. For columns: bit i = 1 means column i has a queen. For left diagonals (constant c-r): index = c - r + N - 1 to make it non-negative. For right diagonals (constant c+r): index = c + r. When placing a queen at (r,c), we set: col_mask bit c, ld_mask bit (c-r+N-1), rd_mask bit (c+r). To check conflicts, we OR these masks. Intuition: This compresses O(N) boolean checks into O(1) bitwise operations, drastically improving performance. Time: O(1) for updates and checks. Space: O(1) per mask. Connection to L2: These masks are state components in iterative DFS. Common mistakes: Incorrect diagonal indexing or not masking to N bits. Real code pattern: col_mask |= 1 << c; ld_mask |= 1 << (c - r + N - 1); rd_mask |= 1 << (c + r).

examples.input: Place queen at (r=0,c=0) on N=4 board, initial masks all 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial masks: col_mask=0000, ld_mask=0000, rd_mask=0000. Step 2: For queen at (0,0): col_mask set bit0: 0000 | 0001 = 0001. Left diagonal index = 0-0+3=3, set bit3: 0000 | 1000 = 1000. Right diagonal index = 0+0=0, set bit0: 0000 | 0001 = 0001. Result: col_mask=0001 (col0 occupied), ld_mask=1000 (left diag3 threatened), rd_mask=0001 (right diag0 threatened).

examples.input: Place queen at (r=1,c=2) on N=4 board, given col_mask=0001, ld_mask=1000, rd_mask=0001
examples.trace: Step 1: Current masks: col_mask=0001, ld_mask=1000, rd_mask=0001. Step 2: For queen at (1,2): col_mask set bit2: 0001 | 0100 = 0101. Left diagonal index = 2-1+3=4, but N=4 so we have 2N-1=7 bits? Actually we use N bits with offset: index=4, set bit4: since we use 4-bit masks, this is out of range—common simplification is to use N bits for all, but for N=4, left diags indices 0-6. In practice, we use integer with enough bits. Assume 8-bit: ld_mask=1000 (bit3), set bit4: 1000 | 10000 = 11000. Right diagonal index = 2+1=3, set bit3: 0001 | 1000 = 1001. So updated: col_mask=0101, ld_mask=11000, rd_mask=1001.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitwise Manipulation for Conflict Detection
ladders.desc: Perform bitwise OR, AND, NOT, and shift operations to manage and query bitmasks.
ladders.explanation: Bitwise operations are fundamental for efficient bitmask handling. Key operations: OR (|) combines masks—e.g., occupied = col_mask | ld_mask | rd_mask. AND (&) filters bits—e.g., safe = ~occupied & ((1 << N) - 1) keeps only N bits. NOT (~) flips bits to find safe positions. Left shift (<<) creates bit masks—e.g., 1 << c sets bit c. Right shift (>>) is less used here. To iterate through set bits in safe: while safe > 0: col = safe & -safe (gets lowest set bit), then safe &= safe - 1 (clears lowest set bit). Intuition: These operations allow us to treat integers as arrays of bits, performing multiple checks simultaneously. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: These operations are used to update and query the bitmask representations. Common mistakes: Forgetting parentheses in bitwise expressions or misunderstanding operator precedence. Real code pattern: occupied = cm | lm | rm; safe = ~occupied & ((1 << N) - 1); while safe: lsb = safe & -safe; col = (lsb.bit_length() - 1); safe &= safe - 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute occupied = 0001 | 0010 | 0001 = 0011 (binary). Step 2: Compute mask = (1 << 4) - 1 = 10000 - 1 = 1111. Step 3: safe = ~0011 & 1111. ~0011 = 1100 (in 4-bit: actually ~00000011 in 8-bit is 11111100, but with &1111 we get 1100). 1100 & 1111 = 1100. So safe=1100, bits 2 and 3 set, meaning columns 2 and 3 are safe.
Step 1: occupied = 101 | 010 | 001 = 111. Step 2: mask = (1<<3)-1 = 111. Step 3: safe = ~111 & 111. ~111 = 000 (in 3-bit), 000 & 111 = 000. So safe=0, no safe columns.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Bitmask Basics
ladders.desc: Learn how integers can represent sets of bits and basic bitwise operations.
ladders.explanation: A bitmask is an integer whose binary representation is used as a compact way to represent a set of boolean flags. Each bit position corresponds to an item (e.g., a column on a chessboard), with 1 meaning 'included' or 'occupied' and 0 meaning 'excluded' or 'free'. For example, in a 4-bit system, the integer 5 (binary 0101) represents that items 0 and 2 are included. Key bitwise operations: AND (&): result bit is 1 only if both bits are 1—used for intersection. OR (|): result bit is 1 if at least one bit is 1—used for union. XOR (^): result bit is 1 if bits differ. NOT (~): flips all bits. Left shift (<<): shifts bits left, equivalent to multiplying by 2—e.g., 1 << 3 gives 8 (1000). Right shift (>>): shifts bits right. Intuition: These operations allow efficient set manipulations without loops. Time: O(1) as CPU instructions. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This foundation enables conflict detection via bitmasks. Common mistakes: Confusing bitwise and logical operators, or not considering integer size. Real code pattern: To set bit i: mask |= 1 << i. To clear bit i: mask &= ~(1 << i). To check bit i: (mask >> i) & 1.

examples.input: Represent columns 0 and 3 occupied in a 4-column board
examples.trace: Step 1: We have 4 columns indexed 0 to 3. Step 2: To represent column 0 occupied: set bit0: 1 << 0 = 1 (binary 0001). Step 3: To represent column 3 occupied: set bit3: 1 << 3 = 8 (binary 1000). Step 4: Combine using OR: 0001 | 1000 = 1001. Step 5: Convert binary 1001 to decimal: 1*8 + 0*4 + 0*2 + 1*1 = 9. So integer 9 represents columns 0 and 3 occupied.

examples.input: Given mask=13 (binary 1101), check if column 2 is occupied
examples.trace: Step 1: Mask=13, binary 1101 (for 4 bits: bits 3,2,1,0 are 1,1,0,1). Step 2: To check column 2 (bit2): shift right by 2: 1101 >> 2 = 11 (binary). Step 3: AND with 1: 11 & 01 = 01. Step 4: Result is 1, so column 2 is occupied. Alternatively, compute (mask >> 2) & 1 = (13 >> 2) & 1 = 3 & 1 = 1.

testCases.input: Set bits 0 and 1 in a 4-bit mask
Flip all bits in mask 5 (101) for 3 bits
Create mask with bits 1,2,3 set for 4 bits
